experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 3 is Evil. The proposed team [0,3] includes a known Evil player, so approving would risk an immediate failed quest and gives cover to Evil. In round 1, rejecting bad teams is standard and does not reveal too much beyond reasonable suspicion.
Player 1: No concrete role information yet from round 1; any two-player team could be good or contain evil. As a good Servant in the first vote, approving a reasonable opening team helps generate quest information, and rejecting without evidence gives little value. My vote reveals I am open to early information-gathering rather than trying to stall.
Player 2: As a Servant, I have no private role information yet. On round 1 with no quest history, the standard good play in 5-player Avalon is usually to approve many opening teams unless they are clearly suspicious, because information is scarce and early approvals avoid unnecessary rejection cycles. The proposed 2-person team [0,3] excludes me, but that alone is not enough to reject. Approving reveals little and helps move the game forward; rejecting without evidence gives minimal benefit.
Player 3: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; others are unknown. The proposed team is [0,3], which includes me but not my teammate. On quest 1 with a 2-person team, as evil I generally want an evil player on the team, and approving a team with me on it gives evil immediate control of the quest outcome. Voting patterns also matter less this early; approving is standard and not too revealing since leaders often support their own team.
Player 4: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, so team [0,3] contains one evil. As evil on an early 2-person quest, approving a team with my teammate is strong because if it passes, 3 can choose to fail and put evil ahead immediately. Even if 3 chooses to pass for cover, having evil on the team is good for us. My vote reveals little since evil can plausibly approve a leader's first proposal.

Player 0: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil. Quest 1 team [0,3] failed with one fail, which is consistent with 3 being evil; I know 4 is also evil, so the proposed team [0,1,4] definitely contains an evil player and is very likely to fail. In team voting, good should reject any team containing a known evil. Rejecting also avoids helping evil score a second point while not strongly outing me, since others can reasonably object based on prior failure patterns too.
Player 2: Quest 1 team [0,3] had exactly one fail, so at least one of 0 or 3 is evil. As a Servant, I want to avoid teams containing both uncertain players and especially avoid auto-approving a team anchored on a suspected player. Proposed team [0,1,4] includes 0, who is suspicious from the failed 2-player quest. While 1 and 4 are untested, approving gives evil a good chance to score again. Rejecting pressures for a team centered away from 0 and/or 3 and preserves information. My vote signals suspicion on 0, which helps good coordinate more than it helps evil.
Player 1: Quest 1 team [0,3] failed with exactly one fail, so at least one of 0 or 3 is evil. As a good Servant, I know I am good, so this proposed team [0,1,4] includes only one player from the failed team (0) and swaps out 3 for 4, which is a reasonable attempt to test which of 0/3 is evil. Approving helps generate information and could succeed if 0 is good and 3 was the saboteur; rejecting gives less information at this stage.
Player 4: Player 3 is confirmed evil to me, and the first failed quest team [0,3] means player 0 is more likely good than evil because only one fail was used; still, 0 could be evil choosing to hide, but with 5 players and only teammate 3 known evil, the safest evil play is to avoid approving a team with only one evil unless necessary. Current proposed team [0,1,4] includes me as the sole known evil member, so if approved I can fail quest 2 and put evil at 2-0. As evil, I want this team to go through. My approve vote slightly supports my own proposal and does not reveal much since leaders often approve their own teams.
Player 3: I know player 4 is evil with me. Quest 1 team was [0,3] and failed with exactly one fail, which strongly implicates me to observant players but doesn't identify 0. Now proposed team [0,1,4] includes my evil teammate 4 and excludes me, so it gives evil a strong chance to fail quest 2 while also making 4 look suspicious only if the quest fails. As evil, I want this team to go through because it contains one guaranteed evil and can secure a 2-0 lead. My approve also aligns with backing a team containing my teammate.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil, so the only possible good players are 0, 1, and 2. The proposed team [1,2] contains no known evil and excludes me, which is fine because a 2-person good team is exactly what we want after two failed quests. As good, I should approve a team that is maximally likely to succeed. Approving reveals some confidence in 1 and 2, but that is acceptable because we urgently need a successful quest and this is the strongest available team.
Player 4: I know Player 3 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 team [0,3] had exactly one fail, which is consistent with 3 failing and says little about 0. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] also had exactly one fail; since I am evil and would likely fail, that suggests 0 and 1 may be good, though double-fail avoidance also matters. Now proposed team [1,2] excludes both known evils 3 and 4, so from evil perspective this is dangerous because it could succeed and give good information. In team voting as evil, optimal play is usually to reject clean teams that exclude all evils, especially with evil already up 2-0 and only needing one more failed quest. Rejecting also avoids confirming 1 and 2 together as good. My vote reveals limited info because good players may also reject for suspicion reasons, but approving a no-evil team risks helping good.
Player 3: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0,1,2 are good. Quest 1 on team [0,3] failed due to my fail, and quest 2 on [0,1,4] failed due to 4's fail. So players 0 and 1 each have been on exactly one failed quest, while 2 has been on none and is certainly good. Current phase is team voting on [1,2], a two-good team, which would likely pass the quest and give Good a point. As evil, I want to prevent good-only teams from going and keep suspicion spread. Rejecting is optimal. My vote against a clean-looking team may be somewhat suspicious, but stopping a likely success helps evil more, and there are enough others who may also reject based on prior failures involving 1.
Player 1: Two failed 2-player quests give strong info. Quest 1 team [0,3] had exactly one evil among 0/3. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] had exactly one fail; since I am good, exactly one evil is among 0/4. Combining these, 0 is the most likely common evil, making 3 and 4 more likely good. Proposed team [1,2] excludes likely good clears and includes unknown 2; in a 2-player quest with game at 0-2, approving a team with one known good and one unknown is too risky. As good, optimal play is to reject and push for teams built around the more-cleared players. My reject signals suspicion of 2 and of excluding 3/4, which helps good coordination more than approving a high-risk team.
Player 2: I’m Good with limited hard info. Quest 1 team [0,3] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0/3 is evil. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] also had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0/1/4 is evil. Player 0 appears on both failed quests, making 0 the strongest evil suspect. Proposed team [1,2] excludes 0, which is good, but includes 1 who was on the second failed quest. With 3-player Avalon, there are 2 evil total; if 0 is evil, the second evil is among 1/3/4. Team [1,2] has a meaningful chance to be all-good if 1 is not the partner. As Good in team voting, I should approve teams that improve over prior failed ones and gather info while avoiding the strongest suspect. Approving [1,2] is better than rejecting a team that excludes 0, and my vote reveals willingness to test away from the main suspect, which helps Good more than it hurts.

Player 0: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil. Proposed team [1,2] excludes both known evils, so it is the best available 2-person team and should be approved. Approving reveals little beyond supporting an all-unknown team, which helps Good gather info and avoids guaranteed evil inclusion.
Player 4: I know 3 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 on [0,3] had exactly 1 fail, which could easily be 3 failing while 0 is good. Quest 2 on [0,1,4] had exactly 1 fail, which I likely provided, making 0 and 1 look more good. Team [1,2] contains no known evil, so if approved it is likely to pass and give Good momentum. As evil on team voting, optimal play is to reject clean teams without evil when possible. My reject also aligns with preserving suspicion patterns since approving a likely-good team would help Good more than it helps my cover.
Player 1: Two failed quests with exactly one fail each. Quest 1 team [0,3] contained at least one evil among 0/3. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] also had exactly one evil; since I am good, at least one evil among 0/4. The overlap points strongly to 0 being evil, because that explains both fails cleanly. That leaves 3 and 4 more likely good, though one of them could be the second evil if 0 were somehow good, which is unlikely. Proposed team [1,2] excludes the strongest evil suspect 0 and tests unknown 2 with me, a known good. As good in team voting, I should approve teams that avoid likely evil and can generate info. Approving helps my team more than rejecting here.
Player 3: I know player 4 is evil with me; players 0 and 3 on quest 1 produced exactly one fail, consistent with me failing and 0 unknown/good. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] also had exactly one fail, likely from 4, making 0 and 1 look more good. Proposed team [1,2] excludes both known evils, so from evil perspective this is dangerous because it could succeed and give good momentum/info. In team voting, optimal evil play is usually to reject all-good teams when possible, especially with evil already up 2-0. My reject signals little since suspicion can be justified by uncertainty around 2, and stopping this team helps evil most.
Player 2: I am Good with limited info. Quest 1 team [0,3] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0/3 is Evil. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0/1/4 is Evil. The overlap makes 0 highly suspicious, though not confirmed; if 0 were Good then Evil would likely be split among {3} and {1,4}. Current proposed team is [1,2], excluding 0 and 3 and including me (known Good). Since only one player besides me is uncertain, this is one of the better possible 2-player teams and worth approving. As Good in team voting, I should support teams that maximize chance of success and gather useful info. Approving [1,2] reveals confidence in excluding the most suspicious player and can test player 1 directly; rejecting delays with no stronger alternative known.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [1,2,3] includes known evil player 3, so for Good this team is highly unsafe, especially on quest 4 when Evil already has 2 fails and only needs one more failed quest to win. Approving would risk immediate loss. My vote reveals some suspicion of 3, but rejecting is necessary to protect Good.
Player 1: I am Good Servant. Known info: Quest 1 [0,1,4] failed, so at least one of 0/1/4 is evil; since I know I am good, at least one of 0 or 4 is evil. Quest 2 [1,2] succeeded, so 2 is very likely good with me. Proposed team [1,2,3] also succeeded on Quest 3, so 3 is also likely good. For team voting on Quest 4, approving a team composed entirely of the successful Quest 3 team is optimal for Good. My vote supports a high-confidence good team and reveals consistency with the strongest available evidence.
Player 3: I am Minion with teammate 4. Team is [1,2,3] and includes me but not 4. Quest 3 with [1,2,3] succeeded, so at least that team composition can be framed as good; however as evil on quest 4, I can fail if approved and win immediately for evil since evil already has 2 quest fails. Approving a team with myself on it is optimal because it gives me direct control to fail the quest. My vote to approve is also consistent with wanting teams I am on to pass through.
Player 2: I’m Good with limited info. Team [1,2] succeeded on quest 3, so players 1 and 2 are currently the best-cleared pair. Earlier failed teams were [0,1,4] and then [1,2], meaning player 4 is highly suspicious from the first fail, while the fail on [1,2] implies either 1 is Evil or that quest history/approvals make 1 uncertain. Adding 3 to the successful [1,2] team is a reasonable extension and avoids the very suspicious 4. On quest 4 for 5 players, a 3-person team still only needs one fail to fail, so approving the best available team is optimal for Good. Approving reveals confidence in the strongest evidence-based team and does not materially help Evil more than rejecting would.
Player 4: I know player 3 is evil with me. Quest 3 succeeded on team [1,2,3], which strongly suggests either 1 and 2 are good and 3 chose to pass to avoid exposure, or one of 1/2 could be evil in a deeper bluff, but with only 2 evils total here that is impossible since I know 3 is my only teammate. So 1 and 2 are very likely good. Current team [1,2,3] contains my teammate but not me; since evil already leads 2-1, approving gives 3 a chance to fail and win immediately on quest 4. As evil, optimal play is to approve any team with 3 on it. My vote also reveals little because approving a previously successful team is natural for good players too, so this helps evil without much suspicion.

Passing is optimal and consistent with good alignment.

Quest 2 with [1,2] succeeded, making 1 and 2 somewhat trusted, and now this team [1,2,3] includes me.
